raw_response: {"rating": -2.6, "lean": "Lean Left", "explanation": "Opinion/Editorial advocacy piece. The author consistently endorses stronger federal environmental regulation, praises EPA’s power plant carbon rules, urges additional regulation of existing gas plants, and commits to defend the rule against legal challenges. These choices align with left-leaning policy preferences, warranting a Lean Left rating. Detailed Analysis: (1) Normative imperatives for more federal regulation: the author says EPA \"must fulfill its commitment... to regulate existing gas-fired power plants\" and later that EPA \"must work expeditiously to finalize\" those standards. (2) Positive evaluative framing of the EPA rule: it is a \"step in the right direction,\" strengthened by the author’s organization, and based on a \"strong technical record\"—all in the author’s own voice. (3) Delegitimizing opposition: anticipated lawsuits are framed as \"attacks,\" and the piece offers no rationale from challengers while asserting EPA acted within its authority. (4) Policy preferences consistent with the left: endorses stringent emissions limits, 90% carbon capture, lower capacity factor thresholds, and enforceable shutdown dates for plants that do not comply. (5) Selective sourcing/editorial focus: highlights CATF and NRDC recommendations and added emissions reductions (e.g., \"over 10 million metric tons\") without presenting countervailing industry, state, or consumer-cost perspectives. (6) Framing implementation as beneficial certainty: emphasizes that the rule will \"provide greater certainty to planners\" and guide states toward compliance, reinforcing a pro-regulatory stance."}

parsed_output.explanation: Opinion/Editorial advocacy piece. The author consistently endorses stronger federal environmental regulation, praises EPA’s power plant carbon rules, urges additional regulation of existing gas plants, and commits to defend the rule against legal challenges. These choices align with left-leaning policy preferences, warranting a Lean Left rating. Detailed Analysis: (1) Normative imperatives for more federal regulation: the author says EPA "must fulfill its commitment... to regulate existing gas-fired power plants" and later that EPA "must work expeditiously to finalize" those standards. (2) Positive evaluative framing of the EPA rule: it is a "step in the right direction," strengthened by the author’s organization, and based on a "strong technical record"—all in the author’s own voice. (3) Delegitimizing opposition: anticipated lawsuits are framed as "attacks," and the piece offers no rationale from challengers while asserting EPA acted within its authority. (4) Policy preferences consistent with the left: endorses stringent emissions limits, 90% carbon capture, lower capacity factor thresholds, and enforceable shutdown dates for plants that do not comply. (5) Selective sourcing/editorial focus: highlights CATF and NRDC recommendations and added emissions reductions (e.g., "over 10 million metric tons") without presenting countervailing industry, state, or consumer-cost perspectives. (6) Framing implementation as beneficial certainty: emphasizes that the rule will "provide greater certainty to planners" and guide states toward compliance, reinforcing a pro-regulatory stance.

explanation: Opinion/Editorial advocacy piece. The author consistently endorses stronger federal environmental regulation, praises EPA’s power plant carbon rules, urges additional regulation of existing gas plants, and commits to defend the rule against legal challenges. These choices align with left-leaning policy preferences, warranting a Lean Left rating. Detailed Analysis: (1) Normative imperatives for more federal regulation: the author says EPA "must fulfill its commitment... to regulate existing gas-fired power plants" and later that EPA "must work expeditiously to finalize" those standards. (2) Positive evaluative framing of the EPA rule: it is a "step in the right direction," strengthened by the author’s organization, and based on a "strong technical record"—all in the author’s own voice. (3) Delegitimizing opposition: anticipated lawsuits are framed as "attacks," and the piece offers no rationale from challengers while asserting EPA acted within its authority. (4) Policy preferences consistent with the left: endorses stringent emissions limits, 90% carbon capture, lower capacity factor thresholds, and enforceable shutdown dates for plants that do not comply. (5) Selective sourcing/editorial focus: highlights CATF and NRDC recommendations and added emissions reductions (e.g., "over 10 million metric tons") without presenting countervailing industry, state, or consumer-cost perspectives. (6) Framing implementation as beneficial certainty: emphasizes that the rule will "provide greater certainty to planners" and guide states toward compliance, reinforcing a pro-regulatory stance.
